responses.answer: The 'one calm weekly view' is genuinely appealing. Right now our family schedule lives across WeChat group chats, my husband's Google Calendar, school notices in a folder I never open, and sticky notes on the fridge. The idea of one shared place that both of us actually look at — without needing to forward anything — is the part that speaks to me most.

My main hesitation is whether this actually replaces anything, or just adds another app to check. In practice, our kids' school communicates almost entirely through WeChat groups — not email. If my husband and I still need to monitor those group chats and manually enter events, the friction doesn't go away. I'd also want to understand the data privacy story before connecting anything to school communications.

The Plus tier at $6.99/month feels reasonable if it genuinely reduces coordination overhead — I spend far more than that in time and stress on scheduling mishaps. That said, I'd want to see it work well for at least a month on the free tier before committing. The Pro tier at $11.99 is harder to justify if the email parsing only covers a fraction of what our school actually sends.

Sunday evening is actually decent for us — we tend to do a loose 'week ahead' conversation over dinner anyway. A digest that surfaces the key items would make that easier. My only concern is if it becomes noise: if the digest includes low-priority things alongside must-do items, I'll start ignoring it. It needs to be genuinely curated, not just a calendar dump.

I'm cautiously open to it, mostly because the brief says the user reviews before anything gets added — that safeguard matters a lot to me. Fully automatic calendar changes would be a dealbreaker. But even with review, I'd want to know exactly what data is being read, stored, and shared. As a parent, my instinct is to be conservative with anything touching school information.

summary: The core problem resonates — our family scheduling is genuinely fragmented, and I'd welcome a calmer system. But I have real doubts about whether FocusLoop fits a Shanghai household where WeChat, not email, is how schools communicate. I'd try the free tier to see if the shared calendar alone is worth the habit change, but I'm skeptical the email parsing feature would deliver much value for us specifically.
